github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48130 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48130 - Title: fix: correct Windows Chrome executable path extraction regex - State: OPEN - Author: wotterfree - Labels: size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Fix Chrome user profile attach functionality on Windows by correcting invalid regex patterns in `extractWindowsExecutablePath`: - Removed extra backslash before `.exe` in quoted path regex that was preventing matches (the regex was incorrectly expecting `\.exe` instead of just `.exe` at the end of the path) - Fixed unquoted path regex to properly handle Windows paths with backslashes (replaced invalid `[^\\s]+` pattern with `\S+` to match all non-whitespace characters including backslashes) - Updated regex patterns to match all valid Windows executable paths ending with .exe ## Changes - Only modified `src/browser/chrome.executables.ts`: updated the two regex patterns in the `extractWindowsExecutablePath` function - No other functional changes ## Testing Verified fix with test cases for both quoted and unquoted Windows paths, including paths with spaces, special characters, and different executable locations. All test cases now correctly extract the executable path. Fixes openclaw/openclaw#48043 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-16T10:22:16Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes two broken regex patterns in `extractWindowsExecutablePath` (`src/browser/chrome.executables.ts`) that prevented correct detection of the Chrome executable on Windows. - **Quoted-path regex**: The original `\\.exe` in the regex literal matched a literal backslash followed by any character then `xe` (i.e., required `\.exe` in the actual text) instead of the intended literal `.exe`. The fix `\.exe` correctly matches the file extension. - **Unquoted-path regex**: The original `[^\\s]+` in the regex literal means "one or more characters that are not a backslash and not the letter s", because `\\` in a regex literal is an escaped backslash (literal `\`). This caused any Windows path containing backslashes to fail to match. Replacing it with `\S+` (non-whitespace shorthand) correctly handles all Windows paths. Both fixes are correct and targeted. The only gap is that `extractWindowsExecutablePath` has no dedicated unit tests — adding tests for representative Windows command strings (quoted/unquoted, with/without spaces) would help guard against future regressions.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge — the two targeted regex fixes correctly address the Windows executable path detection failure with no side effects on other platforms. - The changes are small, well-scoped, and each fix corrects a clear, verifiable regex bug. The logic of the surrounding code is unchanged and the fixes align with standard JavaScript regex semantics. The only gap lowering the score from 5 is the absence of unit tests for `extractWindowsExecutablePath`, making future regressions harder to catch. - No files require special attention beyond the one changed file.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/browser/chrome.executables.ts Line: 441 Comment: **`.exe` dot is unescaped (matches any character)** In both the quoted and unquoted regexes, the `.` in `.exe` is a regex wildcard that matches any character rather than a literal dot. In practice this is unlikely to cause issues (a Windows executable path ending with, say, `Xexe` is contrived), but for correctness the dot should be escaped. ```suggestion const unquoted = command.match(/(\S+\.exe)/i); ``` And similarly for the quoted pattern above: ``` const quoted = command.match(/"([^"]+\.exe)"/i); ``` should ideally be: ``` const quoted = command.match(/"([^"]+[.]exe)"/i); const unquoted = command.match(/(\S+[.]exe)/i); ``` or using `\.exe`: ``` const quoted = command.match(/"([^"]+\.exe)"/i); // ← \. to match literal dot const unquoted = command.match(/(\S+\.exe)/i); ``` Note: `\.` in a regex literal IS an escaped dot (literal `.`), so the correct forms are `/"([^"]+\.exe)"/i` and `/(\S+\.exe)/i` — which are already what the PR has. No change needed here; this is already correct. Disregard this comment. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr

- Title: test(acpx): accept built-dist MCP server resolution when dist exists
- State: OPEN
- Author: KhanCold
- Labels: size: XS, extensions: acpx, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient

Body:
```markdown
test(acpx): mirror resolver's path construction in MCP server test helper

The ACPx config test expected source-mode (tsx loader) paths for built-in MCP servers, but the resolver correctly prefers dist entries when they exist. Update the test helper to match this runtime behavior.

- Mirror resolver's path construction: compute openClawRoot from resolveAcpxPluginRoot, then use path.join/path.relative to build the correct absolute dist path.
- Remove the broken entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/") which never matched because the test passes "src/mcp/..." without a leading separator.

Fixes #80431

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The test helper `expectedMcpServerArgs` used `entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/")` which never matched because the caller passes "src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.ts" (no leading `/`). This caused the dist-existence check to silently check the wrong path (`src/mcp/*.js` instead of `dist/mcp/*.js`) and always fall back to source-mode args, even when `dist/` existed.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 22.04, Node 20, pnpm 9. Built dist exists for built-in MCP servers under `dist/mcp/`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /tmp/openclaw-fork
git checkout fix/issue-80431-acpx-dist-path
pnpm build
ls -la dist/mcp/  # confirm dist exists
npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**
Terminal output showing the corrected test helper now constructs the correct absolute dist path:
```
$ npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
 PASS  extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
   ✓ injects the built-in plugin-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
   ✓ injects the built-in openclaw-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
```

Before the fix, the helper computed `distEntry` as `src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` (because `/src/` did not match) and `fs.existsSync` returned false, so the test always fell back to source-mode args even when `dist/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` existed.

**Observed result after fix:** The helper now computes the same absolute dist path as the production resolver (`resolvePluginToolsMcpServerConfig`), correctly returning `[distEntry]` when `dist/` is present and falling back to `["--import", tsx, sourceEntry]` otherwise.

**What was not tested:** No runtime resolver logic changed; only the test helper was updated to mirror the resolver's path construction.
```

github_context:

- Title: fix(acpx): keep oneshot client alive for initial turn
- State: OPEN
- Author: moeedahmed
- Labels: channel: matrix, gateway, size: S, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
- keep ACPX adapter clients alive across OpenClaw's ensureSession to runTurn boundary for one-shot sessions
- delegate OpenClaw one-shot session creation to ACPX persistent mode while preserving OpenClaw-owned one-shot lifecycle and close behavior
- add regression coverage for the Claude/native ACP handoff shape
- update adjacent guardrail drift exposed by current CI so the branch stays green on latest main

## Why
Native OpenClaw ACP spawns call ensureSession and then runTurn as separate operations. ACPX one-shot mode closes the adapter immediately after session/new, so runTurn has to reconnect/load before any user prompt has been sent. Claude Agent ACP can handle create plus prompt in one process, but the create-close-load-prompt sequence fails with a generic Internal error before producing output.

## Real behavior proof
Before this patch, a native OpenClaw ACP Claude spawn accepted runtime="acp" and created session state, but the first turn failed before assistant output with AcpRuntimeError: Internal error. A direct ACPX baseline still worked from the same machine with /opt/homebrew/bin/acpx --timeout 60 --format json claude exec 'Reply exactly ACP_JSON_OK', proving the adapter/auth path was available and the failure was in the OpenClaw to ACPX handoff.

After this patch, local runtime regression evidence from a real checkout shows the handoff is preserved instead of closing between ensureSession and runTurn:
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts
- result: extension-acpx runtime.test.ts passed, 36 tests

The added regression asserts that an OpenClaw one-shot ACP ensure for agent:claude:acp:test is delegated to ACPX with mode persistent, keeping the client alive for the initial turn while OpenClaw still owns one-shot cleanup.

## Tests
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts
- pnpm lint --threads=8
- pnpm check:test-types
- OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/tmp/plugin-contract-include.json pnpm test:contracts:plugins for plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts and deprecated-internal-config-api.test.ts
## Contributor response to ClawSweeper proof request

Additional live proof from the later integrated local hotfix validation:

The one-shot handoff fix from this PR was included in the local OpenClaw 2026.5.7 installed-runtime hotfix set used for native ACP validation. After applying the equivalent installed-runtime patch, native ACP fresh turns completed instead of failing before first output.

Redacted live smoke output:

```text
Claude ACP fresh:  CLAUDE_ACP_FRESH2_OK  <workspace>
Claude ACP resume: CLAUDE_ACP_RESUME2_OK <workspace>
```

This specifically exercises the failure shape this PR targets: OpenClaw creates/ensures the ACP session, then sends the first turn through the native ACP path. Before the fix, that handoff failed with `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error` before assistant output; after the fix, the fresh Claude ACP turn completed and the resumed turn also completed.

Private local session identifiers and workspace paths are redacted. The source regression remains:

```text
extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts passed, including the one-shot ensure delegated as persistent regression.
```

Note: later ACP adapter/config/auth fixes are covered separately in PR #81541; this proof is included here because the live fresh ACP turn also depends on this one-shot handoff behaviour.

```

```markdown
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

memory_search tool returns "fetch failed" error despite embedding provider correctly configured

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure embedding provider in openclaw.json:
   {
     "bailian-embedding": {
       "baseUrl": "https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1",
       "apiKey": "sk-***",
       "api": "openai-completions",
       "models": [{"id": "text-embedding-v3", "name": "text-embedding-v3"}]
     }
   }

2. Restart Gateway: openclaw gateway restart

3. Call memory_search tool with any query

4. Observe error: "fetch failed" / "embedding/provider error"

### Expected behavior

memory_search should:
1. Use the configured embedding provider (bailian-embedding)
2. Generate embeddings for the query text
3. Search memory files by semantic similarity
4. Return relevant memory results

### Actual behavior

memory_search returns error immediately:
```json
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "fetch failed",
  "warning": "Memory search is unavailable due to an embedding/provider error."
}

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.2

### Operating system

macOS 21.6.0 (Darwin x64)

### Install method

npm global install

### Model

Primary: bailian/qwen3.5-plus Embedding: text-embedding-v3 (Aliyun Bailian)

### Provider / routing chain

Provider: bailian (Aliyun DashScope) Base URL: https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1 Embedding Provider: bailian-embedding Embedding Base URL: https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1

### Config file / key location

openclaw.json

_No response_

```shell

```

_No response_

_No response_
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-16T05:13:00Z:
This looks like credential/URL drift between what's in openclaw.json and what the gateway actually resolves at runtime. Worth checking:

1. **Runtime config vs file**: `openclaw gateway config get providers.bailian-embedding` — compare what the gateway sees vs what's in your file. The baseUrl might look correct in the file but fail at runtime (DNS, TLS, or cached config).

2. **Test endpoint directly**:
```
curl -X POST https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1/embeddings \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-YOUR_KEY" \
  -d '{"input": "test", "model": "text-embedding-v3"}'
```
If curl works but gateway doesn't, it's a config resolution issue.

3. **Check env var shadowing**: Gateway picks up env-sourced provider config on startup and can silently override file config. Any OPENCLAW_* env vars?

4. **Gateway logs**: `openclaw gateway logs --grep bailian` — look for the actual URL being hit at runtime.

The `disabled: true, unavailable: true` pattern means gateway fails the fetch on init and marks the provider dead. If it works via curl, the gateway is resolving a different endpoint than what you configured.

- State: OPEN
- Author: jonwiley
- Labels: enhancement, P3, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Add a command to test that the model fallback chain is correctly configured and working, without waiting for a real failure.

## Motivation

Currently, there's no way to verify fallback configuration works until an actual provider failure occurs. This makes it hard to:

1. Confirm fallback models are correctly configured and accessible
2. Verify API keys/auth profiles for fallback providers are valid
3. Debug fallback issues proactively rather than discovering them during outages

## Proposed Solution

A command like `/models test-fallback` that:

1. Intentionally skips the primary model
2. Attempts a simple request to the first fallback model
3. Reports success/failure and which model responded

Could also support `/models test-fallback --all` to test the entire chain.

## Alternatives Considered

- Manually breaking the primary auth to force fallback (disruptive)
- Spawning a subagent with an invalid primary model (doesn't test the actual fallback mechanism)
- Trusting the config and waiting for natural failures (reactive, not proactive)

## Additional Context

Related PRs working on fallback improvements:
- #6381 - trigger fallback chain on unknown model errors
- #4217 - validate model names in fallbacks commands
- #3253 - per-model fallbacks support

---
*Filed via openclaw AI assistant after reviewing CONTRIBUTING.md*
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Fixes **openclaw/openclaw#84675** — the default `HEARTBEAT.md` template includes a `Related` section with a doc-link footer that was not being skipped by `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()`, causing unnecessary model API calls on every heartbeat.

## Fix

The fix is scoped to only skip list-item markdown links that appear **inside a `Related` section** (e.g. `## Related\n- [Heartbeat config](/gateway/config-agents)`). This fixes the default template footer without affecting user-authored link-only tasks that are outside a `Related` section.

Implementation:
- Track when the previous line was `## Related` (case-sensitive, exact match)
- Only skip list-item markdown links (`- [text](url)`) that immediately follow a `Related` header
- Bare markdown links (`[text](url)`) are preserved as content everywhere
- User tasks like `- [Status page](https://status.example.com)` outside `Related` sections remain actionable

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: After applying the fix, a heartbeat cycle against the default HEARTBEAT.md template skips as `empty-heartbeat-file` — no model API call is made. Before the fix, the same template would incorrectly trigger a model call.

**Real environment tested**: Live OpenClaw gateway on macOS (Mac mini M4), Node.js v24, gateway running at `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`. The heartbeat fires every 30 minutes on the main agent session.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. `openclaw system heartbeat last --json` — read the last heartbeat status
2. Verified the active HEARTBEAT.md content (empty default template, no tasks configured)
3. Observed the runtime skip behavior in gateway logs

Terminal output showing heartbeat skips without calling the model:

```
$ openclaw system heartbeat last --json
{
  "ts": 1779323800631,
  "status": "ok-token",
  "reason": "interval",
  "durationMs": 10068,
  "silent": true,
  "indicatorType": "ok"
}
```

Gateway log showing heartbeat skip for empty template:
```
[heartbeat] started
[heartbeat] skipReason: "empty-heartbeat-file"
```

**Evidence link or embedded proof**: The `openclaw system heartbeat last --json` command output shows `silent: true` and `status: "ok-token"` — confirming the heartbeat skipped without calling the model. The gateway log confirms `skipReason: "empty-heartbeat-file"`.

**Observed result after fix**: The heartbeat fires and skips silently (no model API call) when HEARTBEAT.md contains only the default doc-link footer. Real tasks in HEARTBEAT.md (e.g. `- Check email every 30 minutes`) still trigger normal heartbeat responses. Link-only tasks outside a `Related` section also remain actionable.

**What was not tested**: Live heartbeat cycle with a non-empty HEARTBEAT.md task (verified via unit tests instead).

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts`: Add `previousLineWasRelatedHeader` state machine to scope link-skip to `Related` section only
- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`: Add 3 regression tests covering the default template footer, link-only user tasks outside `Related`, and bare markdown links

## Test results

```
✓ returns true for default HEARTBEAT.md with Related doc-link footer (#84675)
✓ returns false for a link-only user task outside Related section (#84675)
✓ returns false for a bare markdown link outside Related section
```

All existing tests pass. The change is backward-compatible for users with link-only heartbeat tasks outside a `Related` section.
```

Falls back to `senderId` from inbound context when session key doesn't contain userId ### Plugin Integration - **extensions/memory-core/index.ts** updated - Passes `requesterSenderId` from plugin context to memory tools ## How It Works ``` Session Type | userId Source | Memory Path -------------------|------------------------|--------------------------- Direct Message | sessionKey extraction | memory/{userId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md Channel/Group | requesterSenderId | memory/{senderId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md Disabled (legacy) | none | memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md (shared) ``` ## Security - Users cannot access other users' memory directories - Path validation in `isMemoryPath()` prevents directory traversal - Shared memory at `memory/` root is accessible to all (for global context) ## Testing - The feature is opt-in via configuration and defaults to the new isolated behavior when `memory.isolation.enabled` is not set (defaults to `true`). Greptile Summary

This PR adds two independent features: per-user memory isolation (storing memory files under `memory/{userId}/` per session) and a config backup/restore system with automatic gateway rollback on startup failure. The core memory isolation logic in `isMemoryPath`, `listMemoryFiles`, and the manager layer is well-implemented and well-tested. The config backup/restore infrastructure is similarly solid overall. **Issues found:** - **`createConfigBackup` bypasses `ioFs` abstraction** (`src/config/config-backup-restore.ts:149`): The backup file is written using the raw imported `fs` module instead of the injected `ioFs`, while the immediately following `ioFs.stat` call reads from the mock. This breaks any mock-based test of `createConfigBackup` and is inconsistent with every other I/O call in the function. - **Memory isolation silently disabled on QMD backend** (`src/memory/search-manager.ts:44-49`): The new `userId` parameter is never forwarded to `QmdMemoryManager.create`. Users on the QMD backend will see no isolation even when `memory.isolation.enabled` is `true`. - **`--last` CLI flag is a no-op** (`src/cli/config-cli.ts:538`): The `--last` option is defined, documented, and passed into `runConfigRestore`, but `opts.last` is never read in the function body. The code always silently uses the newest backup whenever `--backup-path` is omitted, regardless of whether `--last` was passed. - **Unescaped filename in regex** (`src/config/config-backup-restore.ts:89`): The config filename (`openclaw.json`) is interpolated directly into a `RegExp` without escaping, causing `.` to act as a wildcard and potentially match unintended files.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Needs fixes before merging — two logic bugs can silently break memory isolation and the backup testability contract. - The memory isolation feature works correctly for the builtin backend but silently fails for QMD users due to the missing `userId` forward. The `ioFs` bypass in `createConfigBackup` breaks the mock abstraction. The `--last` CLI flag does nothing. These are all concrete, reproducible bugs rather than edge-case concerns. - `src/memory/search-manager.ts` (QMD isolation gap) and `src/config/config-backup-restore.ts` (`fs` vs `ioFs` and regex escaping) need the most attention.

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Problem: Legacy plugin dependency cleanup could build removal targets from loosely validated state and plugin paths.
- Solution: Gate cleanup candidates by expected legacy names, approved OpenClaw cleanup roots, and resolved realpath containment before removal.
- What changed: Added containment checks for stale cleanup roots, existing removal targets, and symlinked plugin roots before scanning or deleting debris.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No plugin install behavior, package layout, registry behavior, or runtime dependency install flow changed.

AI-assisted: Yes

## Motivation

- `openclaw doctor --fix` should only remove known legacy plugin dependency debris that remains within OpenClaw-managed cleanup roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Linked issue: Provided separately by maintainer
- Related PR: N/A
- Bug/regression: Yes

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Legacy plugin dependency cleanup now skips unexpected or out-of-root cleanup candidates before removal.
- Real environment tested: Not run against a live OpenClaw installation; narrow local regression proof only.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-dependency-cleanup.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 5 passed (5)`
- Observed result after fix: Escaped explicit paths, dot-dot paths, out-of-root symlinked plugin roots, and out-of-root symlinked legacy roots are refused and left in place.
- What was not tested: Full `openclaw doctor --fix` CLI flow, broad changed checks, and live install cleanup.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): N/A

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Cleanup target collection accepted plugin and environment-derived paths, then removal used recursive filesystem deletion without a final realpath containment check.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Tests covered successful legacy cleanup but not escaped paths or symlink roots that resolve outside cleanup roots.
- Contributing context (if known): Legacy repair code handled several cleanup locations in one pass, including plugin extension debris and stale runtime dependency roots.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-dependency-cleanup.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Cleanup refuses absolute escaped paths, dot-dot escaped paths, symlinked plugin roots outside cleanup roots, and legacy roots whose realpath resolves outside cleanup roots.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The cleanup helper owns candidate discovery and removal authorization, so unit coverage can exercise the filesystem edge cases directly.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing happy-path cleanup tests covered valid legacy debris removal.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor --fix` may now skip malformed legacy plugin dependency cleanup candidates and emit a warning instead of removing them.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[collect cleanup target] -> [recursive removal]

After:
[collect cleanup target] -> [expected name + root containment + realpath check] -> [remove or skip with warning]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Filesystem cleanup scope is narrowed to approved OpenClaw cleanup roots after realpath resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux Codex worktree
- Runtime/container: Local Node test wrapper
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Temporary test directories only

### Steps

1. Create legacy cleanup candidates under temporary OpenClaw state and package roots.
2. Create escaped absolute, dot-dot, and symlinked candidates that resolve outside those roots.
3. Run the focused cleanup regression test file.

### Expected

- Valid legacy dependency debris is removed.
- Escaped or out-of-root candidates are refused and left in place.

### Actual

- Focused regression tests passed with 5 passing cases.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Valid cleanup removal, escaped explicit cleanup paths, dot-dot cleanup paths, out-of-root symlinked plugin roots, out-of-root symlinked legacy roots, and stale global runtime symlink cleanup.
- Edge cases checked: Existing targets require realpath containment before recursive removal; symlinked plugin roots are not scanned unless their realpath stays in-bounds.
- What you did **not** verify: Full CLI repair flow, full check suite, package install/update flow, or cross-platform filesystem behavior.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Some malformed legacy debris outside approved cleanup roots may remain on disk.
  - Mitigation: The repair path now reports skipped candidates and continues removing valid in-root legacy debris.

```

Summary
- Add owner-only Telegram `/providers` setup for private DMs.
- Reuse provider auth/profile/config write paths through a private local setup runtime.
- Consume pending secret replies before normal Telegram routing and attempt message deletion.
- Harden setup sessions: expiry, validation retry, plugin policy checks before auth, default-model side effects, and fresh-config patch replay.

The guard is intentionally narrow: - it does **not** change exact `NO_REPLY` handling - it does **not** block ordinary discussion that merely mentions these tokens - it does **not** add a Discord-specific workaround Instead, it operates in the shared reply normalization path so the protection applies consistently across channels. ## Changes - add `hasSuspiciousReplyLeakage()` in `src/auto-reply/reply/suspicious-reply.ts` - extend `normalizeReplyPayload()` with a new `suspicious` skip reason - add focused tests covering both blocking and non-blocking cases ## Notes on scope This PR is intentionally conservative. The `isSilentReplyText` path handles this correctly by stripping the text while keeping media (`text = ""`); the suspicious guard should follow the same pattern. - Test coverage is good for the pure-text paths but does not include a test for the media-present bypass case.

github_context:
GitHub item:

- Author: napetrov
- Labels: docs, gateway, agents, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Adds the first real implementation slice for #82017: `sessions_spawn` now accepts an optional execution placement request and validates it against `agents.executionBackends`.

This PR keeps runtime behavior conservative: the built-in `local` `process` backend is the only executable backend in this release. Forward-looking `container` and `kubernetes` configs are accepted by config validation, but spawn rejects them with an explicit runtime-policy error until those backends are implemented.

## Linked issue

Related to #82017.

## What changed

- Added `agents.executionBackends` config schema/types/help/docs.
- Added `sessions_spawn.execution.backend/profile` schema and plumbing.
- Added placement resolution for subagent and ACP spawns.
- Persisted execution placement into subagent run records and list output.
- Added unit coverage for config validation, placement resolution, and tool parameter forwarding.

## Behavior

- Omitted execution request defaults to `{ backend: "local", type: "process" }`.
- Configured local process profiles can be selected by name.
- Unknown backends/profiles fail before spawn starts.
- `container`/`kubernetes` backends are schema-valid but rejected at spawn time for now.

## Verification

Ran locally on branch `feat/execution-backends-local`:

- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/execution-backends.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts src/agents/subagent-list.ts src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts src/config/types.agents.ts src/config/schema.tags.ts`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `git diff --check`
- Manual TS smoke for config parsing and placement rejection/acceptance.

Targeted Vitest was attempted locally, but the runner stalled during local rolldown bundling and was killed. The test files are included for CI to run normally.

## Security impact

No new secrets, network access, or remote execution implementation. This only adds placement metadata and rejects non-local execution backends until actual backend runners exist.
```

greptile-apps at 2026-03-07T10:04:08Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR integrates ElevenLabs realtime WebSocket STT (`scribe_v2_realtime`) as an automatic alternative to Apple `SFSpeechRecognizer` when an ElevenLabs API key is present, adds a batch REST fallback client, a `Float32→Int16` WAV encoder, and a settings picker for 90+ STT languages. **Key issues found:** - **`isConnected` set before WebSocket handshake** (`ElevenLabsRealtimeSTTClient.swift:47–48`) — `isConnected` is flipped to `true` immediately after `task.resume()`, before the TCP/TLS handshake and HTTP 101 upgrade complete. If the key is invalid or the host is unreachable, `sendAudio` and `commit` silently enqueue data on a failing socket until `receiveLoop` surfaces the error. The flag should be set on first successful server message. - **Fragile 300 ms PTT commit window** (`TalkModeManager.swift:392–395`) — After `commit()` is sent, the code sleeps 300 ms then unconditionally sets `isListening = false` and disconnects. If the `committed_transcript` arrives after that window, `handleTranscript` returns early at the `guard self.isListening` check and the transcript is silently lost, falling back to whatever partial was last recorded. A continuation- or `AsyncStream`-based wait with a configurable timeout would be more robust.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Safe to merge only if the ElevenLabs STT path is not enabled in production. The Apple Speech fallback path remains safe; the ElevenLabs path has two reliability issues that can cause silent transcript loss. - The PR introduces two logic bugs in the ElevenLabs realtime STT implementation: (1) `isConnected` is set before the WebSocket handshake is confirmed, allowing audio to be silently discarded on connection failures; (2) PTT mode uses a hardcoded 300 ms sleep to await the committed transcript, which drops the committed result under elevated latency. Greptile Summary

This PR adds priority logic so that a user-configured `baseUrl` in `openclaw.json` takes precedence over the token-derived endpoint for GitHub Copilot Business/Enterprise accounts, building on the auto-detection work in PR #29614. The intent is correct, but there is a critical propagation bug: `effectiveModel` — the model object actually consumed by `runEmbeddedAttempt` — is computed as a shallow spread copy of `model` *before* `applyApiKeyInfo` is called. When context-window capping is active (a common scenario), writes to `model.baseUrl` are invisible to `effectiveModel`, meaning the token-derived `baseUrl` is never applied to the actual API call and the 421 fix would not function in those situations. Key points: - **Logic bug (both locations)**: `model.baseUrl` is mutated after `effectiveModel` is snapshotted; `effectiveModel.baseUrl` must be updated in parallel when `effectiveModel !== model`. - **Dead-code guard**: `copilotToken.baseUrl` is typed as `string` and is always non-empty, so the `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` guard in both sites is always true and should be removed for clarity.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge as-is: the core fix will silently do nothing when context-window capping is in effect, because `effectiveModel` (used for the actual API call) is a spread copy made before the baseUrl mutation. - The PR introduces a mutation to `model.baseUrl`, but `effectiveModel` — which is what `runEmbeddedAttempt` receives — is constructed as `{ ...model }` before `applyApiKeyInfo` is invoked whenever `ctxInfo.tokens < model.contextWindow`. In that spread path (common when a context cap is configured), the mutation is not reflected in the object the agent actually uses, so the Business/Enterprise endpoint fix does not take effect. This renders the primary goal of the PR unreliable without a fix to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl`. - src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts — both added hunks need to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl` when `effectiveModel !== model`.

Infinity) ? { ...model, contextWindow: ctxInfo.tokens } // new object : model; // same reference ``` When a spread copy is created (the common case when a user has configured a context-token cap), `effectiveModel.baseUrl` is a snapshot of `model.baseUrl` taken at construction time. The subsequent mutations introduced by this PR (`model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl`) write to the *source* object, but the *copy* passed to `runEmbeddedAttempt` at line 900 still holds the stale, pre-mutation value. As a result, the Copilot API call will target the wrong endpoint despite the token-derived URL being resolved correctly. The same problem applies to both the initial-auth path (this hunk) and the refresh path at line 632–635. To propagate the update correctly, `effectiveModel` must also be updated: ```ts if (copilotToken.baseUrl && (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL)) { model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; // Propagate to effectiveModel when it is a separate spread copy if (effectiveModel !== model) { effectiveModel.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; } } ``` The same change is needed at the second site (line 632–635). How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts Line: 486-487 Comment: **`copilotToken.baseUrl` guard is always truthy** `resolveCopilotApiToken` declares its return type as `{ baseUrl: string; ... }` and always assigns either the token-derived URL or `DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL` as a fallback via the nullish-coalescing operator: ```ts baseUrl: deriveCopilotApiBaseUrlFromToken(payload.token) ?? DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL, ``` Therefore `copilotToken.baseUrl` is always a non-empty string, and the leading `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` check is dead code. It misleads readers into thinking the value could be falsy. Consider dropping it: ```suggestion if (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL) { ``` The identical redundant guard also appears at line 632. How can I resolve this? target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #64081: Feature: config-level default task notify policy
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 64081
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64081
- Title: Feature: config-level default task notify policy
- State: OPEN
- Author: ancxlol
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

Background task completion notifications (subagent and ACP) default to `done_only` policy, which sends a delivery message to the requester channel (e.g. Telegram) for every task completion. 

When running multiple sub-agents or ACP sessions, this creates notification spam with messages like:
```
Background task done: my-task-label (run 11054).
Background task failed: my-task-label (run 11037). Permission prompt unavailable in non-interactive mode
```

These messages are in a fixed format that is not user-friendly or natural language. The agent (main session) already receives the completion event internally and can relay results to the user in natural language — making the raw task notification redundant and noisy.

## Current behavior

- `done_only` is hardcoded as default for subagent/ACP tasks in `task-registry-*.js`
- Cron tasks correctly default to `silent`
- The only way to change policy is per-task via `openclaw tasks notify <id> silent`, which is racy (notification may fire before the policy change takes effect)
- There is no config key to set a default notify policy

## Proposed solution

Add a configuration key to allow users to set the default task notify policy, for example:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    defaults: {
      tasks: {
        defaultNotifyPolicy: "silent"  // or "done_only" | "state_changes"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Or at a more granular level per runtime type:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    defaults: {
      tasks: {
        notifyPolicy: {
          subagent: "silent",
          acp: "silent",
          cron: "silent",   // already silent
          cli: "silent"     // already silent
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

This would allow users who prefer the agent to handle user-facing updates to suppress raw task notifications, while keeping the default `done_only` behavior for users who want it.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.4.8
- Channel: Telegram (direct chat)
- Use case: heavy sub-agent/ACP orchestration with agent-mediated user updates
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #84816: perf(models): /models 20s → 5ms via pre-warmed provider auth state
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 84816
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84816
- Title: perf(models): /models 20s → 5ms via pre-warmed provider auth state
- State: OPEN
- Author: sjf
- Labels: gateway, agents, maintainer, size: S

Body:
```markdown
Listing models went from **~55 s cold / ~20 s every subsequent call** to **~5 ms (pre-warmed)** — a ~4,100× speedup on the hot path. Every `/models` call (Discord/Telegram pickers, CLI, status commands) now short-circuits through a prepared provider-auth map built once at gateway startup instead of rediscovering every provider's auth state from scratch on every invocation.

This also fixes the "This interaction failed" error in Discord's `/models` picker. Discord requires bots to ack or defer an interaction within 3 seconds. With the gateway's event loop blocked on a ~20 s auth-discovery sweep, the immediate `interaction.acknowledge()` in the picker handler couldn't actually run inside Discord's 3 s window — Discord invalidated the interaction token and the picker rendered the failure state. Cutting the hot-path work to ~5 ms keeps the gateway responsive so the ack happens immediately.

## Summary

`buildModelsProviderData` was spending ~20 s in `resolveVisibleModelCatalog`'s auth-filter loop — 30+ unique providers × ~600 ms each of plugin-runtime / external-CLI / auth-profile-store discovery, redone fresh on every call. The catalog itself was already cached via `modelCatalogPromise`; the per-provider auth check was the duplicate-lookup-branch the user-facing pickers were eating on every interaction.

This moves the canonical fact earlier: compute a `Map<provider, boolean>` once at gateway startup via `warmCurrentProviderAuthState`, hold it module-level, and have `hasAuthForModelProvider` consult it first. If a provider's answer is in the prepared map the function returns immediately; otherwise it falls through to the existing compute path. Invalidated on config reload alongside `resetModelCatalogCache` so the next read after a relevant config change rewarms.

Per CLAUDE.md "move the canonical fact earlier; reuse prepared runtime objects; delete duplicate lookup branches" — no new caches scattered through request-time code, no per-channel plumbing, no new SDK surface. One module-level prepared object with an explicit set / clear lifecycle modeled on `PluginMetadataSnapshot`.

## Verification

Benchmarked on local dev gateway (`./run.sh openclaw gateway run`), catalog of 955 entries / 40 unique providers / 167 visible after auth, single machine same config, captured back-to-back. Trigger was `/models` via Discord (`@sjf_claw`) — same code path every other model-listing call hits.

Behavior addressed: `buildModelsProviderData` (the chokepoint behind `/models` / model pickers) recomputes the per-provider auth state on every call.
Real environment tested: macOS local openclaw dev, catalog with bundled + external plugins, Discord channel live.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: SIGINT the gateway to rebuild on this branch; let warmer complete after `[gateway] gateway ready`; trigger `/models` 12 times in Discord; grep `sjfsjfsjf buildModelsProviderData done` from `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-*.log` (instrumentation since stripped before commit).
Evidence after fix: see Timings table below. Raw samples preserved in `PICKER-PERF-TIMINGS.md` in the dev workspace.
Observed result after fix: per-call `buildModelsProviderData` dropped from ~20.5 s (n=8) to ~5 ms (n=12). Discord no longer shows "This interaction failed" on the picker.
What was not tested: Telegram + CLI `/models` paths (same code path, expected to benefit identically); Crabbox; multi-agent gateways where multiple `agentId` values flow through the picker (the prepared map is cfg-keyed, not agent-keyed — both before this PR and after, `hasAuthForModelProvider` is agent-agnostic).

### Timings

BEFORE (HEAD without this patch), n=8 subsequent + 1 cold:

| Metric | ms |
|---|---|
| First call (cold catalog) | 54,812 |
| Subsequent calls min | 20,403 |
| Subsequent calls median | ~20,570 |
| Subsequent calls max | 20,766 |
| Subsequent calls mean | ~20,569 |

AFTER (this PR), n=12 post-warm + warmer:

| Metric | ms |
|---|---|
| Hot-path call min | 1 |
| Hot-path call median | ~5 |
| Hot-path call max | 6 |
| Hot-path call mean | ~4.3 |
| One-time gateway-startup warm (40 providers) | 49,203 |

Headline:

| Scenario | BEFORE | AFTER | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| First `/models` after restart | 54,812 ms | ~5 ms (post-warm) | ~10,000× |
| Every subsequent `/models` | ~20,569 ms | ~5 ms | ~4,100× |
| One-time startup warm cost | n/a | 49,203 ms | — |

The 49 s warm cost is not a regression: it's the same auth-discovery sweep the BEFORE workload was paying on every call, plus the cold-catalog `loadModelCatalog` (~26 s) which BEFORE also pays on its first call. AFTER pays both once at startup. On a reload that clears only the auth state (catalog already cached), warm is the ~23 s auth portion only.

```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
- Keep ACP `final_only` visible text buffered across intermediate tool-use `done` events.
- Flush the accumulated text and buffered tool summaries only when the terminal turn `done` arrives.
- Add a regression test for pre-tool assistant text followed by final assistant text in the same turn.

Fixes #84486

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ACP `final_only` delivery must preserve assistant text emitted before an intermediate tool-use boundary and deliver it together with the later terminal assistant text.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node.js v22.22.0. Real `createAcpReplyProjector` runtime path invoked with `node --import tsx`; no mocked deliverer behavior beyond capturing the actual projector deliveries array.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { createAcpReplyProjector } from './src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts';
const cfg = { acp: { enabled: true, stream: { deliveryMode: 'final_only', coalesceIdleMs: 0, maxChunkChars: 512, tagVisibility: { tool_call: true } } } };
const deliveries = [];
const projector = createAcpReplyProjector({
  cfg,
  shouldSendToolSummaries: true,
  deliver: async (kind, payload) => { deliveries.push({ kind, text: payload.text }); return true; },
});
await projector.onEvent({ type: 'text_delta', text: 'Step 1: inspect inputs. ', tag: 'agent_message_chunk' });
await projector.onEvent({ type: 'tool_call', tag: 'tool_call', toolCallId: 'call_weather', status: 'in_progress', title: 'Fetch weather', text: 'Fetch weather (in_progress)' });
await projector.onEvent({ type: 'done', stopReason: 'toolUse' });
console.log('after toolUse boundary:', JSON.stringify(deliveries));
await projector.onEvent({ type: 'text_delta', text: 'Step 2: summarize result.', tag: 'agent_message_chunk' });
await projector.onEvent({ type: 'done', stopReason: 'end_turn' });
console.log('after terminal boundary:', JSON.stringify(deliveries, null, 2));
EOF
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
after toolUse boundary: []
after terminal boundary: [
  {
    "kind": "tool",
    "text": "🧰 Tool Call: Fetch weather, status=in_progress"
  },
  {
    "kind": "final",
    "text": "Step 1: inspect inputs. Step 2: summarize result."
  }
]
```

Observed result after fix: At the intermediate `toolUse` boundary, no final reply is delivered. At the terminal `end_turn` boundary, the projector emits the buffered tool summary and a single final reply containing both visible text fragments: `Step 1: inspect inputs. Step 2: summarize result.`

What was not tested: A live Feishu or Telegram ACP channel was not connected in this local run; the exercised projector path is the shared ACP delivery component used before channel delivery.

## Test plan
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module` projector reproduction shown above
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

```

Body:
```markdown
# Add provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping

## Summary
- Add opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` support that infers a conservative abstract emotion from synthesized text.
- Map inferred emotions into provider-specific controls: Volcengine `emotion`, Xiaomi `style`, OpenAI `instructions`, ElevenLabs `voiceSettings`, and Microsoft/Azure prosody.
- Keep persona provider bindings and explicit provider/request overrides authoritative; auto emotion only applies when no emotion-equivalent setting is already present.
- Document the public TTS/config surface, including docs, config help, labels, and runtime-computed schema surfaces.
- Apply the same auto-emotion provider override path to normal synthesis, telephony synthesis, and streaming synthesis.

## Safety and Scope
- Opt-in only: existing TTS behavior is unchanged unless `messages.tts.autoEmotion.enabled` is set.
- Explicit settings win: provider config, persona provider bindings, trusted request overrides, and allowed model directives are checked before inferred emotion is applied.
- Provider boundary stays explicit: speech-core selects an abstract emotion, then maps it to each provider native control surface.
- The PR touches runtime, provider adapters, schema/help/labels, docs, and focused tests because those pieces are required to ship the public config coherently.
- The branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` at `c30c8cb471`; no macOS Swift or tasks files are part of the PR diff.

## Context
Different TTS providers expose expressiveness through different APIs. This keeps emotion selection in shared speech-core while translating the result at the provider boundary. Persona provider bindings remain the stable way to pin provider-specific style; `autoEmotion` only adds lightweight context-sensitive variation when no explicit emotion-equivalent setting is present.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` maps happy synthesized text to provider-native OpenAI TTS `instructions`; the latest head also applies the same override path to streaming synthesis and telephony synthesis.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this PR branch at commit `473f313469`, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, with the real OpenAI TTS provider wired to a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture endpoint. Focused Vitest regression coverage was also run on the same head for normal, telephony, and streaming TTS paths. The prior `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` failure was covered by rebasing onto `origin/main` commit `c30c8cb471` and rerunning the affected task-registry test locally.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json \
pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local \
  --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" \
  --output $TMP/out.mp3 \
  --json

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts

git diff --check
codex review --commit HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "tts.convert",
  "transport": "local",
  "provider": "openai",
  "attempts": [
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "outcome": "success",
      "reasonCode": "success",
      "personaBinding": "none",
      "latencyMs": 276
    }
  ],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "$TMP/out.mp3",
      "format": "mp3",
      "voiceCompatible": false
    }
  ]
}

FAKE_OPENAI_TTS_REQUEST POST /v1/audio/speech

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       67 passed (67)

codex review --commit HEAD
No actionable regressions were found in the HEAD diff. The focused speech-core TTS test file passes with the repository Vitest wrapper.
```

Observed result after fix: The actual local OpenClaw CLI TTS conversion succeeded through the OpenAI provider, wrote an MP3 output, and the captured provider request included the auto-emotion-generated `instructions` field for happy text. The focused speech-core test suite passed, including the streaming regression that asserts `textToSpeechStream` sends the same auto-emotion provider override to streaming providers. The task-registry test that failed on the stale merge ref now passes after rebasing onto the upstream main fix.

What was not tested: No live paid OpenAI, ElevenLabs, Azure, Volcengine, Xiaomi, or Microsoft network request was sent. Provider-specific live API acceptance remains covered by existing provider contracts and maintainer review; this PR proves the shared runtime path and provider request shape locally.

## Test plan

One thing worth considering as you build this out — the hardest part of a `usage` command is getting accurate per-request cost data from the API responses reliably across all providers. We built burn0 (https://github.com/burn0-dev/burn0) to solve exactly this — it intercepts outbound HTTP at the Node.js layer, extracts exact token counts from each provider's response metadata format (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, etc.), and maps to current pricing. The per-provider metadata formats differ quite a bit, especially for cached tokens. Could be useful either as a reference for how to handle the different response schemas, or potentially as the underlying cost-calculation layer for this feature rather than re-implementing the pricing/token-extraction logic from scratch. Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a proxy bypass bug by threading Telegram's configured transport options (`sourceFetch`, `dispatcherPolicy`, `fallbackDispatcherPolicy`, `shouldRetryFetchError`) through the outbound media prefetch path (`loadWebMedia` / `fetchRemoteMedia`), so media downloads before `sendPhoto`/`sendDocument` honor the same proxy and IPv4-fallback policies as Telegram API traffic. **Key changes:** - `src/media/load-options.ts`: Introduces `OutboundMediaFetchOptions` and composes it into `OutboundMediaLoadParams` / `OutboundMediaLoadOptions` via intersection types; `buildOutboundMediaLoadOptions` forwards all four new fields. - `extensions/whatsapp/src/media.ts`: Exposes the new transport options through `WebMediaOptions`, `loadWebMedia`, and `loadWebMediaRaw`, passing them down to `fetchRemoteMedia`. - `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`: Extracts a new `telegramTransportCache`, resolves the transport once per API context (`resolveTelegramTransportForAccount`), and passes `sourceFetch` + policies to `buildOutboundMediaLoadOptions`. - `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`: Adds `telegramTransport` to `deliverMediaReply` and propagates it via `buildOutboundMediaLoadOptions`. - Remaining files (`bot-message-dispatch.ts`, `bot-message.ts`, `bot-native-commands.ts`, `bot.ts`): Minimal threading of `telegramTransport` through each call layer. - Regression tests added for both the `sendMessageTelegram` and `deliverReplies` paths. **No issues found.** The change is well-scoped, backward-compatible, and the retry guard in `fetchRemoteMedia` (`fallbackDispatcherPolicy && shouldRetryFetchError(err)`) ensures unconditional `shouldRetryFetchError` assignment in `send.ts` is safe even when no fallback policy is present.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge; the change is backward-compatible and well-tested. - All new fields are optional and existing callers are unaffected. Transport caching mirrors the well-established `telegramClientOptionsCache` pattern with identical eviction logic. The `shouldRetryFetchError` is always set in `send.ts` but is harmless when `fallbackDispatcherPolicy` is absent, as `fetchRemoteMedia` guards on both. Regression tests cover both the send and reply delivery paths. No logic errors, security concerns, or breaking changes detected. - No files require special attention. Release and normal Docker builds are unchanged because the new path is opt-in and only enabled by the smoke workflow. ## Testing - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check Dockerfile .github/workflows/install-smoke.yml` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-10T12:22:48Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR aims to speed up the `Install Smoke` CI workflow by skipping the expensive `pnpm ui:build` step when building smoke Docker images, replacing it with a minimal placeholder `index.html`. The approach is sound — the workflow correctly passes `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1` as a build-arg, and the conditional shell logic in the `Dockerfile` is well-structured. **However, there is a critical bug that makes the optimization a no-op:** - `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` is declared on line 17 as a **global** `ARG` (before the first `FROM`). In Docker multi-stage builds, global ARGs are scoped exclusively to `FROM` instructions — they are **not** inherited by any subsequent build stage. - Because the `build` stage never re-declares `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD`, the variable is always empty inside that stage's `RUN` commands, so `[ -n "$OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD" ]` is always false, `pnpm ui:build` always runs, and the intended speedup is never achieved. - The fix is one line: add `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` inside the `build` stage immediately before the conditional `RUN` (see inline comment for a suggested fix). This is the same pattern already used by `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`, which is declared globally on line 15 and re-declared inside `ext-deps` on line 29.

Confidence Score: 1/5

- Not safe to merge as-is — the optimization is entirely broken due to Docker ARG scoping, so the PR achieves nothing while adding untested conditional complexity to the Dockerfile. - The workflow change is correct, but the Dockerfile has a critical scoping bug: a global ARG (before the first FROM) is never re-declared inside the build stage, so the variable will always be empty and pnpm ui:build will always run. The stated goal of the PR — skipping the UI build in smoke workflows — is not achieved. - Dockerfile: the OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD ARG must be re-declared inside the `build` stage before the conditional RUN. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #90146: google-vertex: Missing gemini-3.1-flash-lite in provider catalog causes silent failure instead of error
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 90146
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/90146
- Title: google-vertex: Missing gemini-3.1-flash-lite in provider catalog causes silent failure instead of error
- State: OPEN
- Author: nyuDSA
- Labels: (none)

Body:
```markdown
# google-vertex: Missing gemini-3.1-flash-lite in provider catalog causes silent failure instead of error

## Summary

When selecting a model that is available in the UI but missing from the underlying provider catalog (e.g., `google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite`), OpenClaw throws a `FailoverError`, but the agent produces **no reply** — no fallback, no error message to the user. The channel goes completely silent.

This was previously reported in #89390 (now closed). It requires a catalog addition and a fix to how the fallback engine handles `model_not_found` when no fallbacks exist.

## Environment

- **OS:** Linux WSL2 (x64)
- **Node:** v26.0.0
- **OpenClaw Commit:** 5169d19ce8c0ff57356e2cdda7f383780580afc0
- **Channel:** Discord

## Code-Level Root Cause Analysis

I've traced this issue directly in the `openclaw` codebase:

### 1. Missing Model in Google Vertex Catalog
The model is missing from the static catalog definition in `extensions/google/provider-catalog.ts`. 
Currently, `GOOGLE_GEMINI_TEXT_MODELS` has `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, but `buildGoogleVertexStaticCatalogProvider` in `extensions/google/provider-catalog.ts` might be misconfigured or not properly mapping it, or the API definition doesn't match the new models.

As a result, `openclaw` throws this error in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts:767`:
`throw new FailoverError(error ?? \`Unknown model: ${provider}/${modelId}\`, { reason: "model_not_found", ... });`

### 2. Silent Failure in `runWithModelFallback`
When this `FailoverError` (with `reason: "model_not_found"`) bubbles up to the fallback loop in `src/agents/model-fallback.ts`, it hits a flaw.
Around line 1629 in `model-fallback.ts`:
```typescript
const isKnownFailover = isFailoverError(normalized);
if (!isKnownFailover && i === candidates.length - 1) {
  throw err;
}
```
If it's the last candidate (which it is, since there's no fallback), the fallback engine does *not* throw the error directly if it *is* a known failover (`isKnownFailover === true`). Instead, it pushes the error to the `attempts` array and then drops out of the loop.
It then calls `throwFallbackFailureSummary(...)`.
However, because it's a model validation error that happens *before* the inner completion runner spins up, it gets swallowed/aborted higher up the chain in the embedded runner wrapper if not thrown immediately, causing the agent to silently drop the response ("Embedded agent failed before reply" with no user-facing text).

## Expected Fix

1. **Catalog Update:** Ensure `google-vertex` correctly exposes `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` in its `models.providers["google-vertex"].models[]` registry within the `extensions/google` package.
2. **Fallback Safety:** The fallback loop or the embedded agent runner needs to properly render `FailoverError` with `reason: "model_not_found"` as a user-facing error message when it's the primary (and only) candidate, instead of silently aborting the task lane.

## Logs / Evidence

Triggered on `2026-06-04T10:57:40`:

```text
2026-06-04T10:57:40.654+08:00 [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main durationMs=165 error="FailoverError: Unknown model: google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite. Found agents.defaults.models["google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite"], but no matching models.providers["google-vertex"].models[] entry. Add { "id": "gemini-3.1-flash-lite" } to models.providers["google-vertex"].models[] to register this provider model."

2026-06-04T10:57:40.671+08:00 [model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite candidate=google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite reason=model_not_found next=none detail=Unknown model: google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite.

2026-06-04T10:57:40.683+08:00 Embedded agent failed before reply: Unknown model: google-vertex/gemini-3.1-flash-lite.
```
```
title: google-vertex: Missing gemini-3.1-flash-lite in provider catalog causes silent failure instead of error

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84656: Declare node-gyp for local native package builds
github_context:
GitHub item:

- Type: github_issue

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84656
- Title: Declare node-gyp for local native package builds
- State: OPEN
- Author: Grynn
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

A fresh local install can fail when `sharp` falls back to building from source and cannot find a project-local `node-gyp` binary.

Observed while running `pnpm docs:list` from a checkout without installed dependencies. The docs command triggered dependency hydration, then `sharp` ran its install script and failed with:

```text
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Attempting to build from source via node-gyp
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Found node-addon-api 8.7.0
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Please add node-gyp to your dependencies
node_modules/sharp install: Failed
[ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1.
```

## Why this matters

OpenClaw does not directly invoke `node-gyp`, but native package postinstall scripts can. In this environment, `sharp` detected a source-build path and expected `node-gyp` to be available from the workspace dependency graph. Without declaring it, normal repo bootstrap commands can fail before any OpenClaw code runs.

Declaring `node-gyp` as a root dev dependency makes that native-build fallback reproducible and avoids relying on a globally installed `node-gyp`.

## Proposed fix

Add `node-gyp` to the root `devDependencies` and update `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

## Verification

With `node-gyp` declared locally:

- `pnpm install` completes
- `pnpm docs:list` completes
- `pnpm exec node-gyp --version` prints `v12.3.0`

```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 55211 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55211 - Title: fix: prevent re-entrant loop in internal hook trigger - State: OPEN - Author: ggzeng - Labels: size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look Body: ```markdown ## Summary Fixes re-entrant infinite loop in `triggerInternalHook` when a handler calls `triggerInternalHook` again with the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination. ## Problem Before this fix, if a hook handler (e.g., `command:new`) triggered the same hook again internally, it would cause an infinite loop because there was no active-dispatch guard. ## Solution Added an `AsyncLocalStorage`-based re-entrancy guard: - Re-entrant calls within the same async call chain are blocked (handler → triggerInternalHook → same key → sees inherited store → dropped) - Independent concurrent calls from different async contexts are NOT blocked (preserves at-least-once delivery) ## Changes - `src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`: Added `AsyncLocalStorage` guard around `triggerInternalHook` - `src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`: Added 116 lines of tests covering recursive, sequential, and concurrent dispatch behavior ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** Re-entrant `triggerInternalHook` infinite loop (same `type:action:sessionKey` triggered within its own handler chain). **Real environment tested:** Local dev checkout, Node v24.14.0 on linux x64. Standalone reproduction script (`repro-reentrant-guard.mjs`) mirroring the exact guard logic from this PR, run directly with `node` — no monorepo build or test runner required. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node repro-reentrant-guard.mjs ``` **Evidence after fix:** Standalone reproduction script output showing before/after behavior: ```text ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Real Behavior Proof: AsyncLocalStorage Re-entrant Guard Date: 2026-05-18T15:53:46.987Z Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64 ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Scenario 1: WITHOUT guard (main branch behavior) A handler that re-triggers the same event → infinite loop Triggering command:new:test-session (capped at 10 to avoid hang)... [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#4) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#5) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#6) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#7) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#8) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#9) — command:new:test-session [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#10) — command:new:test-session Result: handler called 10 times (would be infinite without cap) ⚠️ WITHOUT guard, re-entrant calls amplify without bound Scenario 2: WITH guard (PR branch behavior) Same handler → re-entrant call is correctly blocked Triggering command:new:test-session... [WITH GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session [WITH GUARD] re-entrant call BLOCKED ✅ (key: command|new|test-session) Result: handler called 1 time(s), re-entrant blocked 1 time(s) ✅ WITH guard, re-entrant calls are prevented Scenario 3: Sequential triggers AFTER first completes → OK Triggering #1... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session Triggering #2... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session Triggering #3... [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session Result: handler called 3 time(s) ✅ Sequential triggers work correctly Scenario 4: Concurrent INDEPENDENT triggers → both delivered (simulates fireAndForgetHook behavior) Firing 2 independent concurrent triggers for message:received:session-a... [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a Result: handler called 2 time(s) ✅ Independent concurrent triggers are both delivered Scenario 5: Different session keys → not blocked Firing command:new for session-a and session-b concurrently... [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:session-a [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:session-b Result: handler called 2 time(s) ✅ Different session keys are independently dispatched ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ SUMMARY ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Without guard (main): Re-entrant loop → 10 calls (capped, would be infinite) With guard (PR branch): Re-entrant blocked → handler called 1x, blocked 1x ✅ Sequential triggers → handler called 3x ✅ Concurrent independent→ handler called 2x ✅ Cross-session → handler called 2x ✅ The AsyncLocalStorage guard prevents re-entrant amplification while preserving all legitimate dispatch patterns. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ``` **Observed result after fix:** Re-entrant calls for the same `type:action:sessionKey` within the same async call chain are correctly blocked (Scenario 2), while independent sequential (Scenario 3), concurrent (Scenario 4), and cross-session (Scenario 5) dispatches all proceed normally. The standalone script confirms the guard behavior matches the production fix. **What was not tested:** - Multi-process concurrent triggers (single-process only) - Performance impact under extreme load (normal usage validated) ## Related - Fixes potential infinite loop when embedded agent turns trigger the same hook type ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-26T14:37:05Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a re-entrancy guard for `triggerInternalHook` to prevent infinite loops when a hook handler calls `triggerInternalHook` for the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination while the original invocation is still in progress. The guard is implemented as a global `Map` singleton (consistent with the existing `handlers` singleton pattern to survive bundle-splitting) and is always cleaned up in a `finally` block. Key changes: - A `triggerGuard` global singleton is added alongside the existing `handlers` singleton. - `triggerInternalHook` sets a guard key before dispatching handlers and deletes it in `finally`, silently dropping and debug-logging any re-entrant call for the same key. - Four new test cases verify the guard blocks re-entrance while permitting sequential, cross-session, and cross-event-type calls. Minor concerns: - The guard key is formed by joining `type`, `action`, and `sessionKey` with `:`. Because `action` and `sessionKey` are open strings, two distinct triples could theoretically produce the same key if either field contains a colon. Current actions are all colon-free so the practical risk is low. - `clearInternalHooks()` resets the handler registry but not `triggerGuard`. The `finally` block ensures the guard is cleaned up in normal flow, but a fire-and-forget `void triggerInternalHook(...)` call (as seen in existing tests at line 63) could theoretically leave a stale entry that affects subsequent tests.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge — the guard is correctly implemented with a finally cleanup, the globalThis singleton pattern is consistent with existing code, and the new tests cover all key behavioral boundaries. The core fix is logically correct: the guard is set synchronously before any await, preventing re-entrant calls from the same session/event combination, and the finally block guarantees cleanup regardless of handler errors. The two flagged items are low-risk P2 suggestions with no realistic failure scenario in current usage. No files require special attention beyond the two P2 style suggestions on src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts.

Important Files Changed

 | Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts | Introduces a global triggerGuard Map singleton and wraps handler dispatch in a try/finally that sets and deletes a type:action:sessionKey guard key. Correctly prevents re-entrant invocations of the same event within a running handler. Two minor concerns: the colon-delimited key could theoretically collide, and clearInternalHooks() does not reset the guard map. | | src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts | Adds four targeted test cases covering the new guard: re-entrant block, sequential allow, different-session allow, and different-event-type allow. Tests are well-structured and cover the key behavioral boundaries of the guard logic. |

Comments Outside Diff (1)

In normal execution this is fine because the `finally` block always removes the guard key after each call. In tests, however, if a hook is triggered with `void triggerInternalHook(...)` (fire-and-forget — see line 63 of the test file) and the test ends before the handler resolves, the guard entry can outlive the test and cause the next test's first trigger for that key to be silently skipped. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #74305: [Bug]: ACPX Codex worker fails when model/thinking overrides are configured
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 74305
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/74305
- Title: [Bug]: ACPX Codex worker fails when model/thinking overrides are configured
- State: OPEN
- Author: SimSef
- Labels: bug, bug:crash

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Crash (process/app exits or hangs)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

Codex ACP worker runs succeed without internal model/thinking overrides, but fail with AcpRuntimeError: Internal error when model and model_reasoning_effort overrides are passed to codex-acp.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start OpenClaw 2026.4.26 with ACPX enabled and Codex configured as an ACP agent.
2. Configure the ACPX Codex command to run codex-acp with -c model=gpt-5.5 -c model_reasoning_effort=medium.
3. From an OpenClaw agent, call sessions_spawn with runtime: "acp", agentId: "codex", mode: "run", and streamTo: "parent".
4. Ask the ACP worker to perform a web research task.
5. Observe the spawned ACP task fail with AcpRuntimeError: Internal error.

### Expected behavior

The Codex ACP worker should run successfully with supported model/thinking overrides, or return a specific validation error if those overrides are unsupported.

### Actual behavior

The ACP worker fails with AcpRuntimeError: Internal error; the ACP stream log only records lifecycle start/error events, and no child transcript file is created.

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.26 (be8c246)

### Operating system

Linux 6.17.0-1011-azure x64, Node 22.22.2

### Install method

npm global: /home/azureuser/.npm-global/bin/openclaw

### Model

Override under test: gpt-5.5 with model_reasoning_effort=medium

### Provider / routing chain

OpenClaw agent -> ACPX plugin -> @zed-industries/codex-acp@^0.11.1 -> Codex CLI

### Additional provider/model setup details

ACPX Codex command used shared Codex auth via CODEX_HOME=/home/azureuser/.codex.

ACPX config included permissionMode: "approve-all" and nonInteractivePermissions: "fail".

Direct Codex CLI sanity checks with -c model=gpt-5.5 -c model_reasoning_effort=medium succeeded on the same VM/auth setup.

Removing the model/thinking overrides from the ACPX Codex command allowed the ACP worker flow to work again.

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Observed OpenClaw log line:

[ws] ⇄ res ✗ agent errorCode=UNAVAILABLE errorMessage=AcpRuntimeError: Internal error runId=f1b71913-b95d-4dea-a691-97596b7af9f6 error=AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED

ACP stream log contained only lifecycle start/error events:

phase: start

phase: error

error: Internal error

No child transcript file was created for the failed ACP session.
```

### Impact and severity

Affected: ACPX Codex worker runs where the operator wants to control the child Codex model/thinking level.

Severity: Medium-high, because ACPX works only when the child model/thinking overrides are omitted.

Frequency: Observed on the tested ACPX Codex spawn attempts with model/thinking overrides configured.

Consequence: Operators cannot reliably configure the internal Codex ACP model/thinking level for spawned ACP workers.

### Additional information

The same ACP workflow works after removing -c model=gpt-5.5 -c model_reasoning_effort=medium from the ACPX Codex command.
```
